title: Filling a deep gouge in a wood tabletop with two-part wood filler
subtitle: Use an epoxy or two-part wood filler to repair a deep scratch, chip, or impact gouge
excerpt: How to fill a deep gouge, chip, or impact crater in a solid timber or veneered tabletop using a two-part wood filler or epoxy paste. Covers mixing the product, applying it proud of the surface, and levelling and finishing once cured.
sourceNotes: Practical Furniture Restoration, Julian Worthington (2004). Collins Complete DIY Manual, Albert Jackson and David Day (1989). Ronseal, Bostik and Knauf product technical data sheets (reference format, not reproduced).

glossaryTerms.definition: A filler made from a resin base and a hardener that are mixed together immediately before use. On mixing, a chemical reaction causes the product to cure hard — unlike single-component fillers that simply dry by solvent evaporation. Two-part fillers are denser, shrink less, and sand more cleanly than standard one-part fillers. They are rated for exterior and structural use. Pot life (working time after mixing) is typically 3 to 10 minutes depending on the product and temperature.

The period from mixing a two-part product to when it becomes unworkable. Warm temperatures shorten pot life significantly — in a warm workshop the same product may have half the pot life compared to a cold room. Mix only as much product as can be applied in a single pass during the pot life. A product that has started to harden in the mixing tray will not bond correctly to the substrate.

content.text: A deep gouge in a timber tabletop (from a dropped tool, a metal object, or an impact) is too large for a surface wax stick or a single-component filler, which would shrink, crack, and fall out. A
bonds by chemical cure, not evaporation, so it does not shrink significantly and holds in a deep void. It can be sanded, chiselled, and stained after curing. The critical skill is working within the

Two-part wood filler (Ronseal High Performance, Bostik Wood Filler, or similar). A mixing board (a piece of cardboard or glass). A filling knife. Sandpaper: 120-grit, 180-grit, 240-grit. A sanding block. Staining pens or wood stain in a matching colour (for final colour matching if the surface is stained). A fine artist's brush for the stain touch-in.

Clean any dust, wax, or contamination from the gouge interior. Existing finish in the gouge must be abraded: run 80-grit sandpaper into the recess to key the surface. Remove all dust. The filler must bond to bare or keyed wood, not to a polish or varnish film.

Mix the base and hardener on the mixing board to the ratio specified on the product label; usually by length or weight (e.g., 50 parts base to 1 part hardener). Mix thoroughly for 1 full minute: incompletely mixed product has areas that do not cure and remains permanently soft. Mix only enough for the gouge area; a small marble-sized amount for a 10 mm gouge. Work immediately.

Press the mixed filler firmly into the gouge with the filling knife, pressing into the bottom of the void to eliminate air pockets. Overfill slightly; leave the filler standing 1 to 2 mm proud of the surrounding surface. This is intentional: even a chemical-cure filler contracts very slightly as the reaction exotherms and cools. Overfilling allows for this and ensures the cured surface can be levelled flush. Smooth the surface with the knife blade held almost flat across the fill.

Allow the filler to cure fully; typically 30 to 60 minutes for most two-part products at room temperature, harder in a cold workshop. Sand proud areas with 120-grit on the sanding block, working across the grain at first to level quickly, then with the grain to remove cross-grain scratches. Progress through 180-grit and 240-grit. The filled area will be pale grey or cream. If the surface is stained or has a wood tone, apply a staining pen in the matching shade to the filled area. Apply the surrounding finish over the patch.

attrs.symptom: Filler remains soft in the centre after the stated cure time
attrs.cause: Hardener not mixed through fully; the centre stayed as uncatalysed base.
attrs.fix: Pick out the soft material with the utility knife. Re-apply fresh, thoroughly mixed product to the remaining cavity.

attrs.symptom: Filler sands unevenly — leaving low spots
attrs.cause: Filler was not applied proud enough; shrinkage left a concave surface.
attrs.fix: Apply a second, smaller amount of mixed filler to the concave area, overfill slightly, and sand level after curing.
